executive_summary.one_paragraph: GPR75 is an orphan Gαq-class 7TM GPCR (540 aa) with a canonical extracellular N-terminus and three extracellular loops. Surface-accessibility evidence is weak: topology rests on computational predictions and database annotations; the sole live-cell trafficking datum (BRET in HEK293) used an exogenous signal peptide, confounding endogenous surface inference. Expression is CNS-dominant (brain >> periphery), with secondary sites in pancreatic islets and retina. No validated endogenous ligand exists. Antibody validation is limited. Surface localization is inferred by GPCR class membership rather than direct assay.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: No direct surface-accessibility assay (live-cell non-permeabilized IF, surface biotinylation, or IHC membranous with nonperm specification) is present in the ledger. The BRET-based trafficking evidence (a1_evi_07) used an exogenous cleavable signal sequence rather than the native GPR75 sequence, making it methodologically confounded and explicitly flagged as 'weak/supportive but not definitive.' All remaining surface-topology support derives from computational predictions (a1_evi_01), database annotations (a1_evi_03), and secondary review assertions (a1_evi_02, a1_evi_04, a1_evi_05). IHC observations (a1_evi_09, a1_evi_10) were performed on permeabilized/fixed tissue sections without membrane-specific markers. In aggregate, the ledger provides only db_annotations, review_assertions, and a methodologically caveated BRET read — fitting the 'weak' grade.

expression_observations.context: Mouse pancreatic islet cells detected by IHC and western blot; subcellular localization not resolved

antibodies.name: anti-GPR75
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: GPR75 knockout mice used as negative controls to validate antibody specificity in IHC

expression_observations.context: Mouse CNS neurons detected by IHC combined with RNAscope FISH; subcellular localization not resolved to plasma membrane
GPR75 Rluc8-tagged overexpression in HEK 293 G-protein knockout cells; BRET to PM marker substantially higher than BRET to ER marker, indicating efficient surface trafficking

overexpression.signal_peptide_detail: cleavable signal sequence (common forward primer corresponding to a cleavable signal sequence)

non_surface_expression.context: Mouse pancreatic islet cells — GPR75 mRNA detected by RT-PCR and protein by western blot and IHC (permeabilized, fixed tissue)
Mouse pancreatic islet cells — GPR75 mRNA detected by quantitative RT-PCR
Mouse CNS neurons — GPR75 transcripts detected by RNAscope fluorescent in situ hybridization combined with IHC in fixed, permeabilized tissue sections; GPR75 KO mice used as specificity controls
Mouse hippocampal neurons — GPR75 mRNA abundant, as established by prior study (Speidell et al. 2023)
Mouse CNS (multiple brain regions and spinal cord) vs peripheral tissues (spleen, kidney, heart) — GPR75 mRNA higher in CNS; detected by RNAscope FISH across all CNS areas with regional variation

cell_states.descriptor: Adipocyte-specific GPR75 deletion protects female (but not male) mice from HFD-induced weight gain and adiposity, indicating functionally active GPR75 expression in adipocytes under high-fat diet/obese state with sex-dimorphic effect.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: CNS neurons (GABAergic, glutamatergic, monoaminergic) across multiple brain regions
accessibility_modulation.change: GPR75 mRNA and protein are expressed at significantly higher levels in CNS neurons compared to peripheral tissues. Expression is particularly high in hippocampus, cerebellum, substantia nigra/VTA, locus coeruleus, and raphe nucleus.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface-accessible GPR75 is predominantly available in CNS neuronal lineages. Binders targeting GPR75 will have primary access opportunity in brain tissue; peripheral tissue access is likely minimal.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Human retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and perivascular cells of retinal arterioles
accessibility_modulation.change: GPR75 transcript is restricted to RPE cells and perivascular cells surrounding retinal arterioles in human retina, not distributed uniformly across retinal layers.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: In human retina, GPR75 surface exposure is confined to RPE and perivascular cells. Binders must access the sub-retinal space or arteriole vicinity; photoreceptors are not a target in human.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Mouse retina — photoreceptor inner segments and outer plexiform layer
accessibility_modulation.change: Species-dependent shift in intraretinal GPR75 expression: human GPR75 is in RPE/perivascular cells, while mouse GPR75 is in photoreceptor inner segments and outer plexiform layer.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Species difference in retinal cell-type expression means binder accessibility differs between human and mouse retina; human studies will access RPE/endothelial-adjacent surface, mouse studies will access photoreceptor-layer surface.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Pancreatic islets (beta cells) in mouse and human
accessibility_modulation.change: GPR75 protein and mRNA are expressed in pancreatic islets of both mouse and human. Functional activation by CCL5 elevates intracellular calcium and stimulates insulin secretion, confirming surface-expressed, signaling-competent receptor in beta cells.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: GPR75 is surface-accessible on pancreatic beta cells and functionally responsive to ligand. Binders can engage GPR75 in islets and modulate insulin secretion, relevant for metabolic disease targeting.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Adipocytes under normal diet conditions (lean state, WT mice)
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Adipocytes under high-fat diet / diet-induced obesity, specifically in female mice
accessibility_modulation.change: Adipocyte-specific GPR75 deletion produces 50% reduction in weight gain and adiposity in female mice on HFD but has no effect in males, indicating sex-dimorphic functional engagement of GPR75 in adipocytes under obese/metabolic-stress conditions.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: GPR75 becomes functionally relevant (and thus a viable surface target) in adipocytes specifically under diet-induced obesity and in a sex-dependent (female-biased) manner. Binder efficacy in adipose tissue may be context- and sex-dependent.

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: No co-receptor requirement for GPR75 surface trafficking has been described in the ledger. The BRET panel showed efficient PM trafficking in HEK293 cells without a reported co-receptor, but the exogenous signal peptide construct limits inference. No ledger entry identifies a trafficking chaperone or obligate partner.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: In human retina, GPR75 is restricted to RPE and perivascular cells of retinal arterioles rather than uniform retinal distribution. In mouse retina the pattern shifts to photoreceptor inner segments/outer plexiform layer — a species-dependent subdomain difference. CNS expression is cell-type restricted to neurons (GABAergic, glutamatergic, monoaminergic).

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: GPR75 is a canonical 7TM GPCR with a short N-terminal extracellular domain and three extracellular loops. No large structured ECD exists; accessible extracellular surface is limited to the N-terminus and ECL1–3, consistent with the minimal class for GPCRs.

epitope_masking.rationale: N-glycosylation sites are predicted in the extracellular N-terminus based on sequence analysis (Tarttelin et al. 1999). Glycan masking of the small N-terminal ECD is plausible but no direct experimental masking data appear in the ledger; severity rated low/inferred.

evidence.claim: The original 1999 cloning paper for GPR75 (Tarttelin et al.) used computational sequence analysis to predict 7 transmembrane domains, classifying GPR75 as a GPCR with an extracellular N-terminus. This is the primary topology assertion from the discovery paper.

spans.quote: Protein sequence analysis predicts the presence of seven transmembrane domains, a characteristic feature of GPRs.

evidence.claim: GPR75 is a 540-amino-acid protein encoded by only two exons on human chromosome 2p16. This structural annotation anchors the protein size and gene structure, consistent with a 7TM GPCR topology.

spans.quote: GPR75, first identified by Tarttelin et al. in 1999, is a 540-amino-acid protein with only two exons located on human chromosome 2p16.

evidence.claim: A 2025 review figure (sourced from GPCRdb) annotates GPR75 topology as a canonical 7TM receptor with 3 extracellular loops (ECL1-3) accessible to ligands on the extracellular face, 3 intracellular loops (ICL1-3) coupling to G proteins, an extracellular N-terminus, and an intracellular C-terminus. This confirms the ECD orientation for surface-accessibility purposes.

spans.quote: The transmembrane region of GPR75 consists of 7TM, including 3ECL located outside the receptor and involved in binding ligands; 3ICL located inside the receptor and involved in interacting with G proteins or other signaling molecules to transmit signals; and the N-terminal region, which is located outside the membrane, and the C-terminal region, located inside the cell ( https://gpcrdb.org/protein/gpr75_human , accessed on 12 February 2025).

evidence.claim: GPR75 contains seven transmembrane spanning domains with N-glycosylation sites in the N-terminus (extracellular) and serine/threonine phosphorylation sites in the C-terminus (intracellular), as reported by Tarttelin et al. 1999. The N-terminal glycosylation is consistent with an extracellular N-terminus accessible at the cell surface.

spans.quote: GPR75 contains the characteristic seven transmembrane spanning domains, N‐glycosylation sites in the N‐terminus and several serine and threonine phosphorylation sites in the C‐terminus (Tarttelin et al. 1999 ).

evidence.claim: GPR75 is classified as a 540 amino acid Gαq-class GPCR with no significant homology to other classic GPCRs, qualifying it as a structurally distinct member of the GPCR superfamily. This review-level assertion supports the canonical 7TM surface receptor classification.

spans.quote: G-protein coupled receptor (GPCR) 75 (GPR75) is a 540 amino acid member of the G<sub>αq</sub> class of GPCRs, with no homology with other classic GPCRs.

evidence.claim: In the BRET-based surface-trafficking study (PMC8062009), GPR75 and other GPCR coding sequences were cloned using a common forward primer corresponding to a cleavable signal sequence (exogenous/foreign SP) fused to an Rluc8 tag in pRluc8-N1. The exogenous signal peptide was used for all receptors in this panel; therefore the BRET surface-trafficking evidence for GPR75 should be capped at supportive_indirect tier because the foreign SP could artifactually route a cytosolic protein into the secretory pathway.

spans.quote: For each receptor the coding sequence was amplified with a common forward primer (corresponding to a cleavable signal sequence) and custom reverse primer (corresponding to the receptor C terminus) and ligated into a pRluc8-N1 cloning vector.

evidence.claim: In a BRET-based trafficking panel, most receptors including GPR75 showed substantial BRET to a plasma membrane (PM) marker and less BRET to an ER marker, indicating efficient trafficking of the overexpressed Rluc8-tagged receptor to the cell surface. However, because the construct used an exogenous cleavable signal sequence (not the native GPR75 signal), this evidence is supportive but not definitive for endogenous surface localization.

spans.quote: Most receptors showed substantial BRET to the PM marker, and less BRET to the ER marker, indicating efficient trafficking to the cell surface.

evidence.claim: GPR75 (along with other GPCRs) was coexpressed with Gα subunit and Venus-Gβγ in genome-edited HEK 293 cells lacking endogenous Gs/olf, Gq/11, and G12/13 proteins. This methodological detail describes the overexpression system used to measure surface trafficking by BRET; the construct's exogenous signal sequence is a caveat for interpretation.

spans.quote: Receptors were coexpressed together with a Gα subunit and Venus-Gβγ in genome-edited HEK 293 cells lacking endogenous G s/olf , G q/11 and G 12/13 proteins [ 19 ].

evidence.claim: GPR75 was detected in mouse pancreatic islets by western blotting and immunohistochemistry (IHC), and mRNA expression was confirmed by quantitative RT-PCR. The western blot and IHC are whole-cell/tissue-level protein observations without explicit membrane fractionation; this is non-surface-fractionated protein detection. The IHC in islets suggests protein is present in islet cells but subcellular localization to plasma membrane vs. intracellular compartments is not resolved in this abstract.

spans.quote:
GPR75 is coupled to Gq to elevate intracellular calcium, so we investigated whether islets express this receptor and whether its activation by CCL5 increases beta cell calcium levels and insulin secretion.

Methods

Islet CCL5 receptor mRNA expression was measured by quantitative RT-PCR and GPR75 was detected in islets by western blotting and immunohistochemistry.

evidence.claim: A 2026 neuroanatomy study used RNAscope fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) combined with immunohistochemistry (IHC) to detect GPR75 transcripts in specific neuronal cell types in the mouse CNS. The IHC component could provide protein-level localization data; the RNAscope FISH is mRNA-level. This pairing is methodologically relevant as a non-surface-fractionated protein detection approach (IHC does not resolve PM vs intracellular without specific membrane-marker co-staining).

spans.quote: In this study, we quantified GPR75 mRNA expression in the mouse CNS using RNAscope fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) technology, combined with immunohistochemistry (IHC) to detect GPR75 transcripts in specific neuronal cell types.

evidence.claim: GPR75 knockout (KO) mice were used as negative controls to validate the specificity of the RNAscope FISH hybridization and IHC antibody signals in the neuroanatomy study. This KO validation is a critical methodological control establishing that the IHC and FISH signals observed in wild-type mice are GPR75-specific.

spans.quote: GPR75 knockout (KO) mice were used as controls and specificity of hybridization.

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA is abundant in neurons of the mouse hippocampus, as established by a prior study (Speidell et al. 2023). This is an RNA-level tissue expression observation without paired surface protein detection, qualifying as non-surface expression evidence for the non_surface_expression[] bucket.

spans.quote: We have previously established that GPR75 mRNA is abundant in neurons of the mouse hippocampus (Speidell et al. 2023 ).

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA is expressed across all analysed mouse CNS regions (brain areas and spinal cord) with regional variation in expression levels. This is RNA-level (mRNA) expression data without surface protein localization, qualifying as non-surface expression evidence for the non_surface_expression[] bucket.

cell_context.tissue: central nervous system (multiple brain regions + spinal cord)

spans.quote: All analysed CNS areas express GPR75 mRNA; however, there are clear fluctuations in the amount of expression, especially when comparing between brain areas and spinal cord (Figure 1B ).

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA expression is significantly higher in mouse CNS tissues (brain, spinal cord) compared to peripheral tissues (spleen, kidney, heart), consistent with the Human Protein Atlas expression pattern. This is RNA-level tissue-distribution data without surface protein localization, feeding the non_surface_expression[] bucket.

cell_context.tissue: CNS vs peripheral tissues (spleen, kidney, heart)

spans.quote: Further, GPR75 mRNA expression is significantly higher in the CNS when compared to peripheral tissues, such as spleen, kidney and heart (Figure 1C ), and follows a pattern similar to that previously described in the Human Protein Atlas.

evidence.claim: The BRET methodology described involves monitoring direct interactions between GPCRs and G proteins by measuring energy transfer between Rluc8-fused receptors at the cell surface and Venus-tagged G protein heterotrimers in living cells. This is a methodological description of the BRET-based surface detection approach applied to GPR75 in the panel, using live-cell measurements without permeabilization.

spans.quote: We have previously monitored direct interactions between GPCRs and G proteins using bioluminescence resonance energy transfer (BRET) between receptors fused to Renilla luciferase (Rluc8) and G protein heterotrimers tagged with the fluorescent protein Venus.

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA is highly expressed in the human brain, established by Northern blot analysis showing a prominent 7 kb transcript. This is the foundational tissue-expression finding from the original cloning paper.

spans.quote: Northern blot analysis demonstrated a 7 kb transcript highly expressed in the brain.

evidence.claim: In human retina, GPR75 transcript localizes specifically to the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and to perivascular cells surrounding retinal arterioles, as determined by in situ hybridization and Northern blot of RPE. This establishes a restricted intraretinal expression pattern in humans.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: retinal pigment epithelium; perivascular cells of retinal arterioles

spans.quote: In situ hybridisation to human retina and Northern blot analysis of human retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) showed localisation of this transcript to the RPE and cells surrounding retinal arterioles.

evidence.claim: In mouse retina, GPR75 transcript localizes to the photoreceptor inner segments and the outer plexiform layer — a strikingly different intraretinal distribution than in human retina (RPE/perivascular), flagging a species difference in retinal cell-type expression.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: photoreceptor inner segments; outer plexiform layer

spans.quote: In contrast, the transcript was localised to the photoreceptor inner segments and the outer plexiform layer in mouse sections.

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA expression is abundant in the central nervous system (CNS) and significantly higher there than in peripheral tissues such as spleen, kidney, and heart. This pattern is consistent with the Human Protein Atlas.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: CNS vs peripheral tissues (spleen, kidney, heart)

spans.quote: GPR75 expression is abundant in the central nervous system (CNS) more so than in the peripheral tissues; however, much remains unknown about the distribution and role of this receptor throughout the CNS.

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA is expressed across all analyzed CNS regions in mouse, but with clear regional variation; brain regions show higher expression than spinal cord.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: multiple CNS regions including brain areas and spinal cord

spans.quote: All analysed CNS areas express GPR75 mRNA; however, there are clear fluctuations in the amount of expression, especially when comparing between brain areas and spinal cord (Figure 1B ).

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA is abundant in neurons of the mouse hippocampus, confirmed by prior study (Speidell et al. 2023), establishing the hippocampus as a CNS subregion of particularly high expression.

spans.quote: We have previously established that GPR75 mRNA is abundant in neurons of the mouse hippocampus (Speidell et al. 2023 ).

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA expression is significantly higher in CNS compared to peripheral tissues (spleen, kidney, heart) in mouse, mirroring the pattern described in the Human Protein Atlas, corroborating CNS-dominant distribution across species.

spans.quote: Further, GPR75 mRNA expression is significantly higher in the CNS when compared to peripheral tissues, such as spleen, kidney and heart (Figure 1C ), and follows a pattern similar to that previously described in the Human Protein Atlas.

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA is expressed in multiple neuronal populations within the mouse CNS, including GABAergic and glutamatergic neurons across brain regions.

spans.quote: Our results show that GPR75 mRNA expression occurs in several neuronal populations including GABAergic and glutamatergic neurons.

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA is highly expressed in monoaminergic neurons in select CNS areas: substantia nigra/ventral tegmental area (dopaminergic), locus coeruleus (noradrenergic), and raphe nucleus (serotonergic).

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: monoaminergic neurons (substantia nigra/VTA, locus coeruleus, raphe nucleus)

spans.quote: In select areas, such as the substantia nigra/ventral tegmental area, locus coeruleus and raphe nucleus, GPR75 mRNA is also highly expressed in monoaminergic neurons.

evidence.claim: GPR75 mRNA is highly expressed in cerebellar neurons, specifically in both GABAergic and glutamatergic populations of the cerebellum, suggesting a role in motor/equilibrium activity.

spans.quote: Moreover, we found high expression of GPR75 mRNA in the cerebellum, in both GABAergic and glutamatergic neurons, suggesting a potential role for this receptor in motor/equilibrium activity.

evidence.claim: GPR75 was originally characterized for its expression in the human retina (Tarttelin et al. 1999; Sauer et al. 2001), establishing the retina as the first described expression site.

spans.quote: One GPCR that is considered an orphan receptor is GPCR 75 (GPR75), a 540 amino acid member of the G αq class of GPCRs, originally characterized for its expression in the human retina (Tarttelin et al. 1999 ; Sauer et al. 2001 ).

evidence.claim: GPR75 protein and mRNA are expressed in both mouse and human pancreatic islets. CCL5 (the putative ligand) is also co-expressed in islets, suggesting an autocrine/paracrine signaling context.

spans.quote:
Glucose homeostasis in lean and obese mice was determined by measuring glucose and insulin tolerance, and insulin secretion in vivo.

Results

Mouse and human islets express GPR75 and its ligand CCL5.

evidence.claim: GPR75 is expressed in pancreatic islets at both mRNA (RT-PCR) and protein levels (western blot and IHC). The study used GPR75 as the candidate CCL5 receptor on beta cells coupled to calcium signaling and insulin secretion.

spans.quote:
GPR75 is coupled to Gq to elevate intracellular calcium, so we investigated whether islets express this receptor and whether its activation by CCL5 increases beta cell calcium levels and insulin secretion.

Methods

Islet CCL5 receptor mRNA expression was measured by quantitative RT-PCR and GPR75 was detected in islets by western blotting and immunohistochemistry.

evidence.claim: In pancreatic beta cells, exogenous CCL5 reversibly increases intracellular calcium via GPR75, dependent on phospholipase C activation and calcium influx. This is a functional assay demonstrating active GPR75-mediated signaling in the beta-cell state.

spans.quote: Exogenous CCL5 reversibly increased intracellular calcium in beta cells via GPR75, this phenomenon being dependent on phospholipase C activation and calcium influx.

evidence.claim: GPR75 activation by CCL5 stimulates insulin secretion from mouse and human islets in vitro and improves glucose tolerance in vivo in both lean mice and the ob/ob model of hyperglycemia/insulin resistance. This demonstrates functional GPR75 expression relevant to metabolic disease context.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: pancreatic islets (mouse and human); ob/ob mouse model

spans.quote: CCL5 also stimulated insulin secretion from mouse and human islets in vitro, and improved glucose tolerance in lean mice and in a mouse model of hyperglycaemia and insulin resistance (ob/ob).

evidence.claim: Loss-of-function GPR75 variants in humans are associated with leanness (genetic association), and Gpr75 null mice are protected from diet-induced obesity (DIO), establishing GPR75 as functionally relevant in adipose/metabolic tissue contexts.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: human population cohort; Gpr75 null mice

spans.quote: Loss of function G-protein coupled receptor 75 (GPR75) variants in humans are associated with leanness, and Gpr75 null mice are protected from diet-induced obesity (DIO).

evidence.claim: Adipocyte-specific Gpr75 deletion in mice implicates adipocyte-derived GPR75 as a functionally relevant source in diet-induced obesity, indicating significant expression in adipocytes in the context of metabolic stress.

spans.quote: Here, we investigated the contribution of adipocyte-derived Gpr75 to DIO.

evidence.claim: Female adipo-Gpr75-/- mice show 50% reduction in weight gain and adiposity on high-fat diet vs WT, while males are unaffected — demonstrating sex-dependent modulation of GPR75 function in adipose tissue under HFD/obese state. This is an accessibility-modulation signal: GPR75 in adipocytes is functionally active in female obese state but not in males on same diet.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: adipocytes; female vs male adipo-Gpr75-/- mice

spans.quote: Female adipo-Gpr75<sup>-/-</sup> mice displayed a 50% (p < 0.001) decrease in weight gain and adiposity compared to WT, whereas male adipo-Gpr75<sup>-/-</sup> gained weight like WT mice.

evidence.claim: GPR75 is highly expressed in the brain and directly binds Gαq to regulate downstream signaling pathways, as stated in the figure description from a structural/functional study.

spans.quote: GPR75 is highly expressed in the brain and directly binds to Gα q to regulate various downstream signaling pathways.

evidence.claim: GPR75 tissue expression is presented across a panel of tissues using RPKM-based RNA quantification, establishing a multi-tissue expression profile. Brain is indicated as the dominant expression site.

spans.quote:
GPR75 Tissue Expression based on RPKM (reads per kilo-base per million reads placed)

Binding 20HETE to GPR75 Leads to Dissociation of Gαq11 from GPR75.

evidence.claim: Most GPCRs, likely including GPR75, show efficient trafficking to the plasma membrane as indicated by substantially higher BRET signal to a PM-targeted marker than to an ER marker. This supports surface localization rather than ER retention.

spans.quote: Most receptors showed substantial BRET to the PM marker, and less BRET to the ER marker, indicating efficient trafficking to the cell surface.

evidence.claim: GPR75 has been identified across various tissues and organs where it contributes to biological regulation and disease progression, as reviewed in a recent comprehensive deorphanization review.

spans.quote: G protein-coupled receptor 75 (GPR75), a novel member of the rhodopsin-like G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) family, has been identified across various tissues and organs, where it contributes to biological regulation and disease progression.

evidence.claim: GPR75 shows negative/no-detection results (indicated by '--') across all three conditions tested in this comparative GPCR expression panel, suggesting absence or very low expression in the cell types/conditions used in this study.

spans.quote: – – – 8 GPR19 Image 1 NS Image 2 23 SSTR2 NS NS Image 1 38 PTGER1 – – – 9 GPR88 Image 2 Image 1 Image 2 24 FFAR1 – – – 39 PTGER4 – – – 10 GPR146 NT NT Image 1 25 GHR – – – 40 QRFPR – – – 11 GPR150 NT NT Image 2 26 GLP1R – – – 41 SSTR3 – – – 12 LPAR1 Image 1 Image 1 Image 1 27 GPR26 – – – 42 SSTR5 – – – 13 MCHR1 Image 1 Image 2 Image 1 28 GPR63 – – – 43 TRHR – – – 14 NMUR2 Image 2 Image 1 Image 1 29 GPR75 – – – 44 15 NPY1R Image 2 Image 1 Image 2 30 GPR83 – – – 45 NT: not tested in this experiment; NS: no significant difference; ↑: up-regulation; ↓: down-regulation; --: negative results.

confidence_reasoning: A1 grades evidence as 'weak': no direct surface-accessibility assay (live-cell non-permeabilized IF, surface biotinylation, or membranous IHC with non-permeabilization specification) is present. The sole live-cell trafficking datum (BRET) used an exogenous signal peptide, explicitly flagged as confounded. All topology support is computational/db/review. A2 adds tissue-restricted CNS expression and functional islet data but no surface-localization assay. GPR75 remains an orphan receptor (no validated endogenous ligand; a2_evi_11, a2_evi_23), antibody validation is limited (a1_evi_09 — no clone/catalog), and endogenous expression outside CNS is low (a2_evi_23). State dependence is moderate given adipocyte sex-dimorphic and islet disease-context signals. Combined, these factors constrain confidence to low.
